cards.text: To declare a C-style array of 5 ints, write {{c1::int arr[5];}} where the length goes in {{c2::square brackets}}

cards.front: What is the main difference between C-style arrays and std::array?
cards.back: C-style arrays are a core language feature with minimal functionality, while std::array is a standard library class template that provides additional member functions, better safety, and doesn't decay to pointers.

cards.text: To initialize a C-style array with values, use: {{c1::int arr[5] { 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 };}} with braces

cards.front: What happens if you don't initialize a C-style array in local scope?
cards.back: The elements contain indeterminate values (garbage). Unlike std::array with {}, there's no automatic zero-initialization unless you use explicit syntax like = {} or {}.

cards.text: When providing initializers, you can omit the array length: {{c1::int arr[] { 1, 2, 3 };}} and the compiler deduces the size

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: C-style array length must be a compile-time constant. Variable n is not constexpr.
cards.explanation: In standard C++, array lengths must be constant expressions. Some compilers support Variable Length Arrays (VLAs) as an extension, but this is not standard C++.

cards.text: C-style arrays support subscripting with {{c1::operator[]}} just like std::array, and also do {{c2::not}} perform bounds checking

cards.front: Can you assign one C-style array to another using the = operator?
cards.back: No, C-style arrays don't support assignment. You must copy elements individually or use std::copy. This is a major limitation compared to std::array.

cards.text: To get the size in bytes of a C-style array, use {{c1::sizeof(arr)}}, which returns the total bytes, not the element count
To get the number of elements in a C-style array arr, use: {{c1::sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0])}}

cards.concept: Why avoid C-style arrays in modern C++
cards.explanation:
C-style arrays have significant limitations:
  • No bounds checking
  • No built-in size() function
  • Decay to pointers when passed to functions
  • Cannot be assigned or compared directly
  • Limited constexpr support
Prefer std::array or std::vector instead.

cards.front: Can you use range-based for loops with C-style arrays?
cards.back: Yes, range-based for loops work with C-style arrays of known size declared in the same scope. However, they don't work with arrays that have decayed to pointers.
